title: Pallet Cooler Stand
summary: A 2x4-framed cooler housing clad in reclaimed pallet boards, with a hinged, glued-on cooler lid built into the top.
description:
This build wraps a standard cooler in a 2x4 frame skinned with pallet wood, and the sequencing matters more than the joinery: build the bottom support height around your actual cooler before you commit to a final fastener, since the plan flags that this dimension is worth leaving loose until you can test-fit the real cooler. Attach it too early on a hunch and you risk a lid that doesn't clear, or a cooler that rocks on its supports.

The lid is the one place this project genuinely departs from normal woodworking — you're gluing the cooler's own plastic lid to a wood frame with a construction adhesive rated for that kind of bond (the plan specifically calls out Loctite PL Premium or an equivalent polyurethane adhesive, not standard wood glue, which won't hold plastic to wood). Keep nail lengths short wherever pallet boards go on — pallet stock is often thinner than dimensional lumber, and a nail that's too long will punch straight through and split the face.

tools.note: For any cutouts needed to fit the cooler's shape and drain spout.

materials.name: Standard cooler
materials.note: Test-fit before finalizing the frame's bottom support height.

materials.name: Pallet boards
materials.note: Salvaged or purchased — clads the outside of the frame and the lid.

materials.name: Polyurethane construction adhesive
materials.note: Rated for bonding plastic to wood (e.g. Loctite PL Premium) — standard wood glue will not hold the plastic cooler lid to its wood frame.

materials.name: Finish nails, short
materials.note: Pallet stock is often thinner than dimensional lumber — use short nails to avoid punching through.

cutList.note: Height set after test-fitting the actual cooler.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four leg posts at 36", two top frame short rails at 20", four frame long rails at 26" and five bottom support slats at 17" from the Pine or fir. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to four 8 ft Pine or fir. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build two leg sets
steps.body: Attach legs to the top boards with 2-1/2" screws, framing nails, or pocket holes driven from the underside (1-1/2" pocket hole setting, 2-1/2" pocket screws). Build two identical leg sets this way.

steps.title: Connect the leg sets into a frame
steps.body: Attach the 26" support boards to the two leg sets to form the cooler frame, using screws, angled nails, or pocket holes (1-1/2" setting, 2-1/2" pocket screws). Position the bottom boards at a height that will let the actual cooler rest on supports tied into these boards — see the next step.

steps.title: Fit the bottom supports to your cooler
steps.body: Attach the bottom support slats to the frame, setting their height so the cooler's top — including whatever decking material covers it — sits flush with the top of the frame. This is worth test-fitting your actual cooler before committing; you may prefer to leave these supports for a later step once you can confirm the fit exactly.

steps.title: Clad the outside with pallet boards
steps.body: Attach pallet boards (or other reclaimed boards) to the outside faces of the frame with nails and glue — keep nails no longer than 1-1/2" so they don't punch through the often-thinner pallet stock.

steps.title: Build and glue the lid
steps.body: Build a 2x4 frame around the cooler's own lid. Attach pallet boards to one face of the frame with glue and smaller nails (under 1-1/2"). Glue the cooler's plastic lid to the wood frame with a polyurethane construction adhesive rated for plastic-to-wood bonding — standard wood glue won't hold this joint.

steps.title: Hang the lid
steps.body: Set the finished lid assembly on top of the cooler and attach with the hinges. Add a towel hook or bottle opener to the outside if you'd like the extra function.

images.alt: Outdoor cooler stand clad in reclaimed pallet boards with a hinged wood-framed lid
